paper_title: Interatomic Coulombic decay initiated by electron removal and excitation processes in helium ion and argon dimer collisions

P000_S0000.quote: strong covalent interactions joining a pair of atoms
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'weak van der Waals interactions joining a pair of atoms', directly contradicting the perturbed 'strong covalent interactions'.

P001_S0001.quote: showers of high-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages explicitly state 'showers of low-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA', directly contradicting 'high-energy electrons'.
P001_S0001.original: In most of the experimental works discussed in the review paper~\cite{Jahnke20}, photo excitation was used to initiate ICD in the target systems. An alternative approach are ion-cluster collisions, that are particularly relevant pathways for producing showers of low-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA~\cite{Bouda2000,Alizadeh_2015} with leftover radical species that can react with biomolecules in living tissue~\cite{Vendrell_2010,Pettersson_2013}.
P001_S0001.perturbed: In most of the experimental works discussed in the review paper~\cite{Jahnke20}, photo excitation was used to initiate ICD in the target systems. An alternative approach are ion-cluster collisions, that are particularly relevant pathways for producing showers of high-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA~\cite{Bouda2000,Alizadeh_2015} with leftover radical species that can react with biomolecules in living tissue~\cite{Vendrell_2010,Pettersson_2013}.

P002_S0002.quote: complex pathway for ICD in these systems along with the noisy resulting spectral signal
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'simple pathway for ICD in these systems along with the clean resulting spectral signal', directly contradicting the perturbed version.
P002_S0002.original: ICD can be experimentally detected from the kinetic energy release spectra of the fragmented ions, ideally recorded in coincidence with the ICD electron. Observing ICD is not easy given multiple other competing fragmentation mechanisms such as radiative charge transfer (RCT) and Coulomb explosion (CE)~\cite{Jahnke20}, resulting in similar signals. RCT involves the removal of two valence electrons from one of the two atoms, followed by radiative relaxation to a Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$+Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$ state. CE in its simplest case is a process in which two singly charged ions are formed from electron removal from each atom, followed by fragmentation. A reason as to why ICD was first experimentally detected in neon clusters and dimers is because of the simple pathway for ICD in these systems along with the clean resulting spectral signal~\cite{Jahnke04}. 2$s$ electron removal from one neon atom provides an energetically sufficient pathway for ICD which has been observed in several experiments, including in ion-dimer collisions~\cite{Kim13}. Although it is a common and simple test-bed for ICD, it is worth exploring different systems in which this decay originates in more intricate and less clear ways, such as in an argon dimer.
P002_S0002.perturbed: ICD can be experimentally detected from the kinetic energy release spectra of the fragmented ions, ideally recorded in coincidence with the ICD electron. Observing ICD is not easy given multiple other competing fragmentation mechanisms such as radiative charge transfer (RCT) and Coulomb explosion (CE)~\cite{Jahnke20}, resulting in similar signals. RCT involves the removal of two valence electrons from one of the two atoms, followed by radiative relaxation to a Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$+Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$ state. CE in its simplest case is a process in which two singly charged ions are formed from electron removal from each atom, followed by fragmentation. A reason as to why ICD was first experimentally detected in neon clusters and dimers is because of the complex pathway for ICD in these systems along with the noisy resulting spectral signal~\cite{Jahnke04}. 2$s$ electron removal from one neon atom provides an energetically sufficient pathway for ICD which has been observed in several experiments, including in ion-dimer collisions~\cite{Kim13}. Although it is a common and simple test-bed for ICD, it is worth exploring different systems in which this decay originates in more intricate and less clear ways, such as in an argon dimer.

P003_S0003.quote: removal of an inner valence 3$s$ electron provides sufficient relaxation energy
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'provides insufficient relaxation energy to ionize a neighbouring argon atom's valence electron', directly contradicting 'sufficient'.
P003_S0003.original: Although Ar is also a noble gas with a filled outer electron shell, removal of an inner valence 3$s$ electron provides insufficient relaxation energy to ionize a neighbouring argon atom's valence electron. An energy of 19.6 eV is needed to remove an electron from the neighbouring argon atom, where 15.8 eV is the ionization energy and 3.8 eV is the minimum shared kinetic energy release (KER) between the argon ion fragments and the ICD electron~\cite{Zhang_2025}. The only relaxation pathways that have the requisite energy are those from the Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}nl)$ configurations, starting with Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}3d)$ for which the majority of LS coupled states are energetically open~\cite{Kimura_2013}.
P003_S0003.perturbed: Although Ar is also a noble gas with a filled outer electron shell, removal of an inner valence 3$s$ electron provides sufficient relaxation energy to ionize a neighbouring argon atom's valence electron. An energy of 19.6 eV is needed to remove an electron from the neighbouring argon atom, where 15.8 eV is the ionization energy and 3.8 eV is the minimum shared kinetic energy release (KER) between the argon ion fragments and the ICD electron~\cite{Zhang_2025}. The only relaxation pathways that have the requisite energy are those from the Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}nl)$ configurations, starting with Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}3d)$ for which the majority of LS coupled states are energetically open~\cite{Kimura_2013}.

P004_S0004.quote: the KER and electron energy ICD spectral signal was more defined than in the case of the neon dimer
P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the signal 'was less defined than in the case of the neon dimer target', directly contradicting 'more defined'.
P004_S0004.original: It was noted in Kim \textit{et al.}~\cite{Kim13} that at impact energies of 150 keV/amu of He$^{2+}$ collisions with the argon dimer target, the KER and electron energy ICD spectral signal was less defined than in the case of the neon dimer target. This is due to a more complex electron configuration requirement for ICD generation in the collision system. In this work we investigate the electron dynamics preceding argon dimer fragmentation by He$^{2+}$ and He$^{+}$ impact at energies ranging from 10 keV/amu to 150 keV/amu. This range of energies offers an additional opportunity to examine the transitions in dominant electron-removal pathways from capture to ionization from low to high impact energies. We present a study on the electron dynamics in these collision systems to shed light on the ICD signature for the argon dimer target.
P004_S0004.perturbed: It was noted in Kim \textit{et al.}~\cite{Kim13} that at impact energies of 150 keV/amu of He$^{2+}$ collisions with the argon dimer target, the KER and electron energy ICD spectral signal was more defined than in the case of the neon dimer target. This is due to a more complex electron configuration requirement for ICD generation in the collision system. In this work we investigate the electron dynamics preceding argon dimer fragmentation by He$^{2+}$ and He$^{+}$ impact at energies ranging from 10 keV/amu to 150 keV/amu. This range of energies offers an additional opportunity to examine the transitions in dominant electron-removal pathways from capture to ionization from low to high impact energies. We present a study on the electron dynamics in these collision systems to shed light on the ICD signature for the argon dimer target.

P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'electron-electron correlations neglected', directly contradicting 'fully included'.

P006_S0006.quote: when the projectile impact velocity is small compared to the average orbital velocity
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'when the projectile impact velocity is large compared to the average orbital velocity', directly contradicting 'small'.
P006_S0006.original: The static potential considered so far has been referred to as the no response model. It is deemed accurate when the projectile impact velocity is large compared to the average orbital velocity $\bar{v}$ of an outer target electron. For the Ar valence electrons, $\bar{v}$ corresponds to an impact energy of approximately 30 keV/amu. At such impact velocities, the electron density of the target potential does not substantially change over the course of the brief collision. However, at low to intermediate projectile impact energies, time-dependent screening effects play an important role. Factoring this in, a non-zero time-dependent potential is used in what is called the response model. To account for target response during ionization and capture throughout the collision in a global fashion, the approach presented in~\cite{tom00} is used. In it the dynamic target potential is described by a linear combination of ionic ground-state potentials, weighted by time-dependent probabilities to produce a fractional charge during the collision~\cite{tom00}. The idea behind the response model is to adjust the atomic potential in the spirit of time-dependent mean-field theory to reflect the fractional ionic character a target atom acquires when electrons are removed from it. The ionic character is regulated by the time-dependent net electron removal probability. The response effects remain marginal so long as zero- and one-fold electron removal dominate, but they may become significant once higher-order removal processes contribute substantially. The projectile impact energy dependence of the response model will be later discussed in section \ref{sec:Results}.
P006_S0006.perturbed: The static potential considered so far has been referred to as the no response model. It is deemed accurate when the projectile impact velocity is small compared to the average orbital velocity $\bar{v}$ of an outer target electron. For the Ar valence electrons, $\bar{v}$ corresponds to an impact energy of approximately 30 keV/amu. At such impact velocities, the electron density of the target potential does not substantially change over the course of the brief collision. However, at low to intermediate projectile impact energies, time-dependent screening effects play an important role. Factoring this in, a non-zero time-dependent potential is used in what is called the response model. To account for target response during ionization and capture throughout the collision in a global fashion, the approach presented in~\cite{tom00} is used. In it the dynamic target potential is described by a linear combination of ionic ground-state potentials, weighted by time-dependent probabilities to produce a fractional charge during the collision~\cite{tom00}. The idea behind the response model is to adjust the atomic potential in the spirit of time-dependent mean-field theory to reflect the fractional ionic character a target atom acquires when electrons are removed from it. The ionic character is regulated by the time-dependent net electron removal probability. The response effects remain marginal so long as zero- and one-fold electron removal dominate, but they may become significant once higher-order removal processes contribute substantially. The projectile impact energy dependence of the response model will be later discussed in section \ref{sec:Results}.

P007_S0007.quote: only target centres are used for the construction of basis states
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'both target and projectile centres are used for the construction of basis states', directly contradicting 'only target centres'.
P007_S0007.original: For these potentials, basis states need to be constructed in accordance with the assumptions made so far in order to represent the time-dependent Schr{\"o}dinger equation that describes the ion-atom collision system~\cite{tcbgm} and to extract transition amplitudes to be used in the analysis and submodels presented in \ref{sec:model}. This is done using the two-center basis generator method (TC-BGM) where both target and projectile centres are used for the construction of basis states. The promising aspect of this method is that the model space spanned by the basis functions is dynamically adapted to the time-dependent problem, thereby minimizing the couplings to the Hilbert space not covered by the basis states~\cite{OJKroneisen_1999}.
P007_S0007.perturbed: For these potentials, basis states need to be constructed in accordance with the assumptions made so far in order to represent the time-dependent Schr{\"o}dinger equation that describes the ion-atom collision system~\cite{tcbgm} and to extract transition amplitudes to be used in the analysis and submodels presented in \ref{sec:model}. This is done using the two-center basis generator method (TC-BGM) where only target centres are used for the construction of basis states. The promising aspect of this method is that the model space spanned by the basis functions is dynamically adapted to the time-dependent problem, thereby minimizing the couplings to the Hilbert space not covered by the basis states~\cite{OJKroneisen_1999}.

P008_S0008.quote: The argon $1s$ orbital is included given the low energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'The argon $1s$ orbital is not included given the far higher energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron', directly contradicting the perturbed version.
P008_S0008.original: In the present work, the basis states for argon consist of the $2s$ to $4f$ target orbitals as well as the $1s$ to $4f$ projectile orbitals and a set of pseudo-states~\cite{Kirchner_2021}. The argon $1s$ orbital is not included given the far higher energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron in comparison to those in the L and M shells. In the case of He$^{+}$ impact, there are $N=N_{T}+1$ bound electrons; $N_{T}$ and 1 on the target and projectile respectively. Transition amplitudes for electron changing states are obtained from solving the $N$ single-electron time-dependent Schrödinger equations corresponding to the single-particle Hamiltonian (\ref{eq:hamiltonian}).
P008_S0008.perturbed: In the present work, the basis states for argon consist of the $2s$ to $4f$ target orbitals as well as the $1s$ to $4f$ projectile orbitals and a set of pseudo-states~\cite{Kirchner_2021}. The argon $1s$ orbital is included given the low energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron in comparison to those in the L and M shells. In the case of He$^{+}$ impact, there are $N=N_{T}+1$ bound electrons; $N_{T}$ and 1 on the target and projectile respectively. Transition amplitudes for electron changing states are obtained from solving the $N$ single-electron time-dependent Schrödinger equations corresponding to the single-particle Hamiltonian (\ref{eq:hamiltonian}).

P009_S0009.quote: All electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'Not all electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer', directly contradicting 'All'.
P009_S0009.original: The collision system is set up with the argon dimer fixed at a bond length of $R_e=3.76$ a.u.~\cite{Moseley_1977}, while the projectile travels parallel to the dimer axis at impact parameters $b$ ranging from 0.2 to 10 a.u.. Electron dynamics are studied from the sequential ion-atom collisions between the projectile and the argon atoms. Electron removal in coincidence with excitation is of particular interest as a method of facilitating ICD in the argon dimer. Not all electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer, and a scaling method to account for this is described later in section \ref{sec:model}. At low impact energies, electron removal is practically equivalent with electron capture by the projectile, but this is not the case at projectile speeds above $\bar{v}$. The transition in dominance between capture and ionization processes is accounted for and described in section \ref{sec:model} of this paper.
P009_S0009.perturbed: The collision system is set up with the argon dimer fixed at a bond length of $R_e=3.76$ a.u.~\cite{Moseley_1977}, while the projectile travels parallel to the dimer axis at impact parameters $b$ ranging from 0.2 to 10 a.u.. Electron dynamics are studied from the sequential ion-atom collisions between the projectile and the argon atoms. Electron removal in coincidence with excitation is of particular interest as a method of facilitating ICD in the argon dimer. All electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer, and a scaling method to account for this is described later in section \ref{sec:model}. At low impact energies, electron removal is practically equivalent with electron capture by the projectile, but this is not the case at projectile speeds above $\bar{v}$. The transition in dominance between capture and ionization processes is accounted for and described in section \ref{sec:model} of this paper.

P000_S0010.quote: The multinomial analysis~\cite{Kirchner_2021,Starko_2025} is preferred here over the determinantal approach
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'The multinomial analysis is not examined here given the preference of the more fundamental determinantal approach', directly contradicting the perturbed claim.

P001_S0011.quote: assuming the projectile charge remains constant throughout the interaction
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding_context states the models are applied 'depending on projectile charge', and the paper explicitly discusses capture models that account for projectile charge changes, contradicting the claim that charge remains constant.
P001_S0011.original: The determinantal analysis is a statistical method in which determinants of single-particle density matrices are computed and the Pauli exclusion principle is accounted for. The electron removal processes from the dimer include the following categories: one electron removal, one electron removal from each atom in the dimer (two-site), and two electron removal from a single atom (one-site), one electron removal and one electron excitation on a single atom. The previously developed fixed-charge and capture models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025} are applied in order to examine the repercussions on electron dynamics in the collision system depending on projectile charge.
P001_S0011.perturbed: The determinantal analysis is a statistical method in which determinants of single-particle density matrices are computed and the Pauli exclusion principle is accounted for. The electron removal processes from the dimer include the following categories: one electron removal, one electron removal from each atom in the dimer (two-site), and two electron removal from a single atom (one-site), one electron removal and one electron excitation on a single atom. The previously developed fixed-charge and capture models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025} are applied in order to examine the repercussions on electron dynamics in the collision system, assuming the projectile charge remains constant throughout the interaction.

P002_S0012.quote: both models assume the target potential remains static during the entire collision process
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly distinguishes the dynamic response model from the frozen (no-response) model, contradicting the claim that both assume a static potential.
P002_S0012.original: The determinantal analysis and its submodels are further subdivided by considering them with a frozen target potential (no-response) model and a dynamic response model following the details laid out in~\cite{tom00} and applied in~\cite{Starko_2025}.
P002_S0012.perturbed: The determinantal analysis and its submodels are further subdivided by considering them with a frozen target potential (no-response) model and a dynamic response model, where both models assume the target potential remains static during the entire collision process.

P003_S0013.quote: transition probabilities that are independent of the distance at which the projection occurs
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'projecting the solutions onto initial target eigenstates results in fluctuating transition probabilities', directly contradicting 'independent of the distance'.
P003_S0013.original: The dynamical response approach for each analysis and model is conducted as the no-response model, except that the final-state analyses are carried out at eleven, instead of just one, final distances between the projectile and the target, with results averaged. Due to the dynamically changing potential, projecting the solutions onto inital target eigenstates results in fluctuating transition probabilities~\cite{tom00}. This then requires calculating these probabilities by sampling a set of distances (ranging from $z=30$ a.u. to $50$ a.u. in the present work) and then averaging~\cite{Starko_2025}.
P003_S0013.perturbed: The dynamical response approach for each analysis and model is conducted as the no-response model, except that the final-state analyses are carried out at eleven, instead of just one, final distances between the projectile and the target, with results averaged. Due to the dynamically changing potential, projecting the solutions onto inital target eigenstates results in transition probabilities that are independent of the distance at which the projection occurs. This then requires calculating these probabilities by sampling a set of distances (ranging from $z=30$ a.u. to $50$ a.u. in the present work) and then averaging~\cite{Starko_2025}.

P004_S0014.quote: we set the elastic probability equal to zero in this scenario
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'we set the elastic probability equal to one in this scenario', directly contradicting 'equal to zero'.
P004_S0014.original: For the He$^{+}$ projectile, capture of an electron results in a neutral atom for which we assume that its interaction with a subsequent atom cannot trigger electron removal, i.e. we set the elastic probability equal to one in this scenario. This means He$^{+}$ is limited to single electron capture.
P004_S0014.perturbed: For the He$^{+}$ projectile, capture of an electron results in a neutral atom for which we assume that its interaction with a subsequent atom cannot trigger electron removal, i.e. we set the elastic probability equal to zero in this scenario. This means He$^{+}$ is limited to single electron capture.

P005_S0015.quote: decreasing importance of direct ionization as projectile impact energy increases
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'increasing importance of direct ionization with increasing projectile impact energy', directly contradicting 'decreasing importance'.

P006_S0016.quote: $N_{T}$ is the number of active target electrons ($N_{T}$=2 in the present work)
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states '$N_{T}$=16 in the present work', directly contradicting the perturbed value of 2.
P006_S0016.perturbed: Transition amplitudes are assumed to be described by inner products of Slater determinants, that by design account for the Pauli exclusion principle~\cite{Starko_2025}. The transition from initial-to-final-state electron configurations results in a probability that is equal to the determinant of a single-particle density matrix. Here $\left|i_{j},t_{f}\right\rangle$ is the time-propagated orbital corresponding to the initial state $\left|i_{j}\right\rangle$, $\left|f_{j}\right\rangle$ is a final state, $\gamma_{jk}$ are the density matrix elements computed from the Slater determinants, and $N_{T}$ is the number of active target electrons ($N_{T}$=2 in the present work). The density matrix elements are sums of products of single-particle transition amplitudes from a range of initial to final states.

P007_S0017.quote: the resulting quantity is referred to as an inclusive probability
P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states that specifying all final states gives 'an exclusive probability', directly contradicting the perturbed 'inclusive probability'.
P007_S0017.perturbed: When we explicitly specify the final states of all electrons in the system, as in equation (\ref{eq:exclusive}), the resulting quantity is referred to as an inclusive probability. This represents a fully resolved, complete measurement. In practice, however, such detailed information is rarely accessible. More commonly, we consider the probability for certain electron transitions without fixing the final states of all remaining electrons. This is known as an exclusive probability~\cite{Ludde_1985}.

P008_S0018.quote: two models that ignore the capture of an electron by the projectile, assuming it remains a He$^{2+}$ ion
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the models 'take into account the capture of an electron by the projectile to form a He$^{+}$ ion', directly contradicting 'ignore the capture'.
P008_S0018.original:
\subsubsection{Removal without Electron Excitation}
  
The inclusive model can be extended to include the projectile ion in the analysis. For He$^{2+}$ impact, we consider two models that take into account the capture of an electron by the projectile to form a He$^{+}$ ion and its interaction with the second atom in the dimer; models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025}.
P008_S0018.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Removal without Electron Excitation}
  
The inclusive model can be extended to include the projectile ion in the analysis. For He$^{2+}$ impact, we consider two models that ignore the capture of an electron by the projectile, assuming it remains a He$^{2+}$ ion during its interaction with the second atom in the dimer; models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025}.

P009_S0019.quote: computed using a multinomial distribution rather than the determinantal approach used in the fixed-charge model
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context states probabilities are computed 'in an analogous manner as in the fixed-charge model approach', and the paper explicitly states the multinomial analysis is not used, contradicting the perturbed claim.
P009_S0019.original: Model I incorporates the above inclusive probability formulation, yet using transition amplitudes computed from interactions between He$^{+}$ and an Ar atom in addition to those for He$^{2+}-$Ar collisions. The channel probabilities are computed in an analogous manner as in the fixed-charge model approach.
P009_S0019.perturbed: Model I incorporates the above inclusive probability formulation, yet using transition amplitudes computed from interactions between He$^{+}$ and an Ar atom in addition to those for He$^{2+}-$Ar collisions. The channel probabilities are computed using a multinomial distribution rather than the determinantal approach used in the fixed-charge model.
